Atty. TIMOTHY GHOBRIAL, Esq. of Downtown LA Law Group (tel: 213-389-3765) for Plaintiff.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Mar-07-2024 Continued To May-21-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Mar-08-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. CHELSIE LIBERTY, Esq. of Kronenberg Law PC (tel: 510-254-6767) for specially appearing defendants Bruce Wyatt and Julie Wyatt.
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT WYATT, BRUCE; WYATT, JULIE's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Bruce and Julie Wyatts' motion to quash service of summons and dismiss the complaint for lack of personal jurisdiction is granted.

Plaintiff Gracie Walters rented an Airbnb cabin in Oklahoma owned by the Wyatts. After slipping and falling in the cabin's shower, Walter sued Airbnb and the Wyatts in this court for negligence and premises liability. The Wyatts are Texas residents. The Wyatts now move to dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction. (CCP 418.10.)

Specific jurisdiction arises if (1) the defendant has purposefully availed itself of forum benefits with respect to the matter in controversy, (2) the controversy is related to or arises out of the defendant's contacts with the forum, and (3) the assertion of jurisdiction would comport with fair play and substantial justice. (Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 536.) The plaintiff bears the initial burden of demonstrating facts justifying the exercise of jurisdiction. (Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 434, 449.)

Walters argues the Wyatts' use of Airbnb, a company with significant California contacts, satisfies the minimum contacts test for specific personal jurisdiction. (Opp. 5:6-7.) To the extent Walters relies on Airbnb's contacts with California, not that of the Wyatts, she misconstrues the standard. (See Calder v. Jones (1984) 465 U.S. 783, 790 ["Each defendant's contacts with the forum State must be assessed individually"].) Indeed, the only contact from the Wyatts that Walters has shown on this record is that the Wyatts used the Airbnb platform by way of their property manager, and so did she. Maintaining an Airbnb listing, however, does not prove the Wyatts purposefully directed their activities towards California residents. No evidence shows the Wyatts targeted California residents. The situation is akin to a minimally interactive website, in that the hosts' Airbnb posting allows travelers to book rentals through information passively made available on their page. (See Pavlovich v. Superior Court (2002) 29 Cal.4th 262, 274.)

In sum, Walters has not carried her burden of establishing either of the first two requirements of specific jurisdiction, and the court need not examine the third.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: Victor Da Fonseca/Jacqueline Alameda; Not reported. (302/RCE)
